replies.author: @annicaesplund6613
replies.text: ​@GlobeDiscovery-NYI live in Sweden.

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: That's a very fair point, and it definitely depends on the country or state. In many places, the average tax bracket stays well under 30% for most people. However, in certain countries (like some European nations) or for high-income earners, the marginal tax rates can easily go beyond 40% or 50%. It's fascinating how much tax structures vary around the world!

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: I'm truly sorry if the video missed the mark for you. Gathering accurate data on a country's daily realities can be tough, and I apologize if some nuances or facts were oversimplified or incorrectly represented here. I deeply value this feedback and will work harder to thoroughly research and verify everything to ensure the highest quality content for our viewers. Thanks for keeping me accountable!

More entertaining than correct; e.g. Police records aren't accessible by the public — nor by other authorities.

comments.author: @guol7791
comments.text: The myth has arisen regarding high suicide rates in Sweden, stemming from transparent honest accurate reporting. In Catholic countries, by contrast, suicide is often viewed as a sin, leading to causes of death other than the actual one being recorded in statistics. Suicide is no more common in Sweden than in other countries.

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: Spot on! Thank you for bringing up this crucial historical and sociological context. The 'reporting bias' is a well-documented phenomenon—countries with stricter religious taboos often misclassified suicides to protect families, whereas Sweden’s data has always been exceptionally transparent. It’s wonderful to have viewers who add such valuable depth to the comment section!

comments.author: @andyPandy2155
comments.text: Restaurants don't close 8 pm in cities. In Stocholm they close 10-12 pm. The Swedish tax is NOT 50%. For 90% of the population it is 30-35%! We have free education, including pre scool and universities. Practically free hospitals including advanced operations. Medicins subdidized. Only very rich pay 50% and IKEA pay ZERO % because of advanced tax planning! Yes I cut my pizzas with a cissor because I fund out it was practical. I didn't know others did it!

replies.author: @magnusk4352
replies.text: You’re wrong about what most swedes pay in income tax. Yes the municipality tax (kommunalskatt) is between 30-35% but you’re forgetting the employer’s tax (arbetsgivaravgift) which 2026 is 31.42%. Total tax lands between 45-55% for employees that’s not paying state tax. Then we pay 25% extra tax when using our salaries to buy things, and on and on it goes…

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: Thanks for dropping all these fantastic insider updates from Stockholm. Things on the ground can evolve fast, and policies or local habits definitely vary depending on the specific city or income bracket. If any details in the video missed the absolute latest shifts or simplified the nuances of the Swedish system, I really appreciate your understanding. Thanks for keeping our community informed.

comments.author: @Plush_RC_Customs
comments.text: #2 is wrong. The tax is about 30%, Not 50%.  Get your facts right.

replies.author: @herrbonk3635
replies.text:
The total tax is way higher than 50%, i.e. including the hidden so called _sociala avgifter_ and _arbetsgivaravgift._
On top of that are (mostly) 25% sales taxes as well as heavy point taxes (60-70%) on things like electricity, petrol and alcohol.

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: Thanks for keeping me on my toes! You're totally right that the basic municipal income tax sits around 30% for most people. The 50%+ figure actually kicks in for high earners due to the national progressional tax (statlig inkomstskatt). Appreciate the clarification—I'll make sure to word this more precisely in future videos!
